serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant plate of stir-fried kai lan in oyster sauce is approximately 200g, typically served as a shared vegetable side dish at a Chinese restaurant or zi char stall.

nutrient_source.title: Stir-fried Kai Lan in Oyster Sauce — HPB Singapore Foodo / Health Promotion Board Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for stir-fried kai lan in oyster sauce. All per-100g values are taken from this verified HPB record. HPB is the primary approved source for Singapore dishes.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Non-Starchy Vegetables — University of Sydney GI Database; Chinese broccoli (kai lan / gai lan) analogous to other Brassica non-starchy vegetables
gi_source.source_match_notes: Kai lan (Chinese broccoli, Brassica oleracea var. alboglabra) is a non-starchy Brassica vegetable. No direct GI measurement for stir-fried kai lan in oyster sauce exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of approximately 18 is estimated by analogy with other non-starchy Brassica vegetables (broccoli, cabbage, kale) which consistently test at GI 10–25 in the University of Sydney database. Glycemic load per 200g serving = (18 × 5.5 × 2) / 100 = 2.0, confirming very low GL. Confidence: 0.9.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (485 mg/100g) from oyster sauce and light soy sauce; a 200g serving delivers approximately 970 mg sodium
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit portion size or request reduced oyster sauce; the vegetable itself is low in sodium and rich in potassium (228 mg/100g), which partially counteracts the sodium load, but the sauce-derived sodium remains a significant concern for blood pressure management.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index (~18) and very low glycemic load per serving (GL 2.0); minimal carbohydrate content (5.5 g/100g) with 1.8 g dietary fibre
type2_diabetes.guidance: Stir-fried kai lan in oyster sauce is an excellent choice for people with type 2 diabetes — the very low GI and GL minimise postprandial glucose excursion, and the high fibre and micronutrient density (vitamin C, folate, calcium) support overall metabolic health; the oyster sauce adds only minimal sugar and starch.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium (485 mg/100g) is a cardiovascular risk factor; however, the lipid profile is favourable — very low saturated fat (0.5 g/100g), zero trans fat, zero cholesterol, and the Brassica glucosinolates and vitamin C may confer cardioprotective benefits
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For cardiovascular disease patients, the high sodium content from oyster sauce warrants caution — request reduced sauce or sauce on the side; the underlying vegetable is cardioprotective with negligible saturated fat, zero cholesterol, and high antioxidant vitamin C (45 mg/100g) and folate (38 mcg/100g).

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory Brassica vegetable profile with high vitamin C (45 mg/100g), beta-carotene/vitamin A (95 mcg RAE/100g), folate (38 mcg/100g), and Brassica glucosinolates
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Kai lan is an excellent choice for rheumatoid arthritis patients — its high vitamin C, beta-carotene, and Brassica glucosinolates support anti-inflammatory pathways, and the calcium (72 mg/100g) supports bone health; zero saturated fat and zero cholesterol further support an anti-inflammatory dietary pattern.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Stir-fried Kai Lan in Oyster Sauce (蠔油芥蘭, Ho Yau Gai Lan) is the definitive Chinese restaurant and zi char stall vegetable side dish in Singapore and across the Cantonese Chinese diaspora. The dish features Chinese broccoli (kai lan / gai lan, Brassica oleracea var. alboglabra) — a robust, dark-green Brassica vegetable with thick crunchy stems, broad deeply-coloured leaves, and a slightly bitter, assertive flavour distinct from Western broccoli. Preparation involves briefly blanching the kai lan in boiling water to set its vivid colour and par-cook the stems, then tossing it in a very hot wok with fragrant fried garlic and a glossy sauce made from oyster sauce, light soy sauce, a touch of sugar, and a cornflour slurry. The result is a vibrant platter of crisp-tender stems and wilted leaves coated in a glossy, umami-rich oyster sauce glaze. Nutritionally, kai lan is one of the most micronutrient-dense Chinese vegetables — providing exceptional vitamin C (45 mg/100g), beta-carotene/vitamin A (95 mcg RAE/100g), calcium (72 mg/100g), folate (38 mcg/100g), iron (1.2 mg/100g), and dietary fibre (1.8 g/100g) — but the oyster sauce preparation introduces substantial sodium (485 mg/100g). Energy density is low at 65 kcal/100g. The glycemic index is very low (~18) with a glycemic load of only 2.0 per 200g serving, making it an excellent choice for people with type 2 diabetes. The high sodium content makes it a concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease patients, who should request reduced sauce. A 200g plate is the standard restaurant serving. Allergens include molluscs (oyster sauce) and soy (light soy sauce); oyster sauce may also contain wheat gluten.
meal_context: shared table vegetable at Chinese banquet

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database — the primary approved source for Singapore dishes — with an exact match for stir-fried kai lan in oyster sauce. All per-100g values are verified. GI is estimated by analogy with non-starchy Brassica vegetables from the University of Sydney GI Database (confidence 0.9). Ontology uses the closest defensible FoodOn and FoodEx2 anchors with honest gap notes.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this stir-fried preparation is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central for this specific dish. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this cooked preparation is not reported in the HPB source. trans_fat_g is confirmed 0.0 per HPB source. cholesterol_mg is confirmed 0.0 per HPB source — this is a plant-based vegetable dish with no animal-derived fat; cholesterol is naturally zero. vitamin_b12_mcg is confirmed 0.0 — naturally zero as this is a plant-based vegetable dish with no animal products contributing B12. vitamin_d_mcg is confirmed 0.0 — naturally zero for a stir-fried vegetable dish with no vitamin-D-containing ingredients. GI of 18 is an analogous estimate based on non-starchy Brassica vegetables in the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement exists for this specific preparation. Allergens: molluscs (oyster sauce), soy (light soy sauce), potential gluten (oyster sauce may contain wheat). Hindi local name provided as transliteration-based equivalent for Singaporean multilingual compliance.
